pain_points.theme: Lack of a structured budget vs. mere expense logging
representative_phrases: Your problem is you don't have a budget. Writing down what you're spending isn't truly tracking it.
Tracking it means understanding where your money is going in comparison to where you want it to go
no app is going ti tell you where your problems are if you don't understand your own priorities
Starting out, unplanned expenses, whether malfortune or indiscretion, often wiped out my savings

pain_points.theme: Confusion over safe cash storage vs. earning interest
representative_phrases: I have 30k roughly. But it's just literally sitting in my night stand (I know. Should be in the bank)
get that $30k out of your nightstand and into a high yield savings account or something like a money market account

article_context.serp_intent: A searcher for "money saving box" typically wants practical tools, ideas, or products like piggy banks, jars, or DIY containers to physically store cash as part of a savings habit or challenge. The SERP intent is primarily commercial and informational, featuring product listings from retailers, DIY tutorials, and guides on using such boxes in money-saving challenges to build discipline.

paa_questions.a: The 10k smash box turns saving into a fun, trackable journey! Each number on the box represents a step toward your $10,000 goal—just mark them off as you go. Watching your progress build makes saving exciting and rewarding! 【The Gift That Keeps on Saving】This money saving box isn't just a piggy bank—it's a challenge!

paa_questions.q: What is a money saving box called?
paa_questions.a: A piggy bank (sometimes penny bank or money box) is a coin container normally used by children, featuring a slot at the top to insert coins or folded bank notes. The piggy bank is known to collectors as a "still bank" as opposed to the "mechanical banks" popular in the early 20th century.

Last Updated: December 15, 2025 Using a Shoebox Using a Plastic Food Container Using Gift Boxes To Make a Wedding Box Q&A Things You'll Need This article was co-authored by Amy Guerrero . Amy Guerrero is an Arts and Crafts Specialist and the Owner of Sunshine Craft Co., a crafting studio based in Phoenix, Arizona. Amy specializes in macrame, DIY crafting, and teaching fiber arts. She offers monthly in-person and online workshops along with having developed a range of DIY craft kits for at-home projects. Amy holds a BS in Industrial Design from Philadelphia University. She worked as a graphic designer before starting her own business. Sunshine Craft Co. is a creative hub that offers a wide range of workshops, tools, and resources for any craft project to inspire creativity and community engagement. This article has been viewed 158,672 times. You can use a money box to serve various purposes, like storing savings, accepting donations for special occasions or fundraising, for keeping spare change in the house, etc. Making a money box is a relatively simple and quick project that can also make a good crafts project for kids. Steps WH.performance.clearMarks('image1_rendered'); WH.performance.mark('image1_rendered'); 1 Draw the coin slot. Take the lid off the shoebox, and, using your largest piece of currency (eg, quarter, half-dollar, etc.) as a guide, draw a rectangular coin slot wide enough for it to fit. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-f31b114fac')); WH.performance.mark('step1_rendered'); You can draw the coin slot on either the lid or one of the long sides of the box, depending on your preference. 2 Cut out the coin slot. Using a craft knife (scissors will leave a ragged edge), cut out the rectangle to make the coin slot. [1] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-d8ebf38bf3')); If a child will be doing this project, have an adult do this part. Advertisement WH.ads.addBodyAd('step2_ad_1') 3 Measure the dimensions of the box. You’ll use these measurements to cut out pieces of paper or fabric to fit the box. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-9a34654b3e')); Use a ruler to measure the height, width, and length of the shoebox on its four sides and its lid. Write these measurements down. Use a ruler to measure the length and width of the top of the lid. Measure the height of the lip of the top and add that onto both the length and width measurements. Write the measurements down. 4 Cut out the paper or fabric. Lay the paper or fabric upside down on a flat surface. Using a ruler, outline the dimensions each of the four sides and the lid. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-d1296ebd01')); Cut out each shape. If necessary, you can label each piece along with the part of the box with which it corresponds s o as to avoid confusion. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/0/09/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-17-Version-3.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-17-Version-3.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/0/09/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-17-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-17-Version-3.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons

After you’ve glued the paper or fabric to the top of the lid, turn the lid upside down and set it on a flat surface. [2] X Research source Use a craft knife to cut out the paper covering the lid’s coin slot. If a child will be doing this craft, have an adult do this part. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/4/4b/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-18-Version-3.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-18-Version-3.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/4/4b/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-18-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-18-Version-3.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons

Begin decorating the pieces of paper with which you’ll cover the box before gluing the paper. Add any desired drawings or writing. Glue on any desired decorations like ribbon, buttons, charms, etc. If the coin slot looks ragged, line it with a ribbon to disguise any roughness. Wait for the glue to dry so the decorations are securely in place before proceeding. 7 Glue on the paper or fabric. Cover the back side of each cutout with a thin layer of glue. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-f5d03aa481')); Press it into place against the corresponding part of the box. When gluing the paper or fabric to the lid, use the additional allowance to drape over the sides of the lid. Wait for the glue to dry. Replace the lid and start using your money box! WH.ads.addBodyAd('method_ad_1') {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/3/36/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-1-Version-4.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-1-Version-4.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/3/36/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-1-Version-4.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-1-Version-4.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons 
 
 ","zoomWidth":728,"zoomHeight":410,"zoomUrl":"/images/thumb/3/36/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-1-Version-4.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-1-Version-4.jpg.webp"} 1 Find a suitable plastic food container with a plastic lid. You’ll want a container large and sturdy enough for your particular purpose that includes a plastic lid, like a can of nuts. [3] X Research source Empty the container of its contents. Wash and dry the inside. 2 Remove the lid and set it flat and upside-down on a cutting surface. Make sure the lid is sitting so that it’s resting flat against the cutting surface for stability. [4] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-9ead2e1aa9')); Find the largest coin you have (like a quarter or half-dollar). Using the coin as a guide, trace the outline for the slot opening with a permanent marker or felt-tip pen . 3 Cut out a hole in the lid. Using the outline you traced, cut a hole for the coin slot with a craft knife or pocket knife (which ever is best). [5] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-1aa5f5ea1a')); Be extremely careful when using the craft knife, as it’s quite sharp. If a child is doing the project, perform this step for them. 4 Test the coin slot. Using the same coin, test the slot you’ve cut in the lid to be sure it’s big enough. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-95f2f7dfe4')); If the slot is too small, carefully use the craft knife to trim the edges until it’s the right size. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/9/9c/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-5-Version-4.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-5-Version-4.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/9/9c/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-5-Version-4.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-5-Version-4.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons 
 
 ","zoomWidth":728,"zoomHeight":410,"zoomUrl":"/images/thumb/9/9c/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-5-Version-4.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-5-Version-4.jpg.webp"} 5 Measure the container. Using a flexible measuring tape or ribbon, measure the container so you can wrap it with the right amount of paper or fabric. Measure the circumference of the container; write down the measurement, adding one or two centimeters for overlap allowance. [6] X Research source Measure the height of the container and write it down. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/9/9b/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-6-Version-4.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-6-Version-4.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/9/9b/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-6-Version-4.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-6-Version-4.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons 
 
 ","zoomWidth":728,"zoomHeight":410,"zoomUrl":"/images/thumb/9/9b/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-6-Version-4.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-6-Version-4.jpg.webp"} 6 Choose the paper or fabric with which to wrap the container. This is what will turn your can from a boring food container to an attractive, personalized money box. Choose or cut out a piece of paper or fabric large enough to wrap all the way around the container. Lay the paper or fabric face-down on your cutting surface. 7 Outline the dimensions of the container. Using the height and circumference measurements you wrote down, use a ruler to draw a rectangle of the same height. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-12a3db2ff3')); Use the circumference (including the additional centimeter or two) for the width of the rectangle. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/f/f1/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-8-Version-3.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-8-Version-3.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/f/f1/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-8-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-8-Version-3.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons 
 
 ","zoomWidth":728,"zoomHeight":410,"zoomUrl":"/images/thumb/f/f1/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-8-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-8-Version-3.jpg.webp"} 8 Cut out the rectangle. After tracing out the rectangle, cut it from the paper or fabric using the outline as a guide. Once the rectangle is cut, wrap it around the container to make sure it fits properly. If it’s too large, trim it down to size. If it’s too small, begin again with a new piece of paper or fabric. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/4/4c/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-9-Version-3.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-9-Version-3.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/4/4c/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-9-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-9-Version-3.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons 
 
 ","zoomWidth":728,"zoomHeight":410,"zoomUrl":"/images/thumb/4/4c/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-9-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-9-Version-3.jpg.webp"} 9 Add any decorative writing or drawings you want to include. Before securing the paper or fabric to the container, lay it down right side up and write or draw any of the decorations you plan to use. It will be significantly easier to perform this step while the paper or fabric is lying flat than when it’s wrapped around the container. 10 Wrap the paper or fabric around the container and secure it. When you’ve finished adding any desired writing or drawings, flip the fabric or paper upside down. [7] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-bcc7887b66')); Spread a thin layer of glue over the entire backside. Slowly and carefully wrap the paper or fabric around the container, smoothing it with your hand as you go. The paper or fabric should ideally overlap, but if it doesn’t and a small strip of the container is visible, cover it with a ribbon, a strip of colored paper, or another decoration. {"smallUrl":"https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/1/16/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-11-Version-3.jpg/v4-460px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-11-Version-3.jpg","bigUrl":"/images/thumb/1/16/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-11-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-11-Version-3.jpg","smallWidth":460,"smallHeight":259,"bigWidth":728,"bigHeight":410,"licensing":" License: Creative Commons 
 
 ","zoomWidth":728,"zoomHeight":410,"zoomUrl":"/images/thumb/1/16/Make-a-Money-Box-Step-11-Version-3.jpg/aid626162-v4-728px-Make-a-Money-Box-Step-11-Version-3.jpg.webp"} 11 Add any additional desired decorations. After the paper or fabric is secure, add any additional decorations you may want. At this stage, you can add decorations like buttons, ribbons, jewels, etc. Use hot glue if the decoration is heavy or won’t stay in place with regular glue. 12 Place the lid on the container. Finally, place the lid with the coin slot on the container. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-e7746e4dd1')); When the glue is dry, your money box will be ready for use. 1 Choose the gift boxes you’ll use. Gift boxes are a slightly nicer option for money box material and so can be ideal for making a wedding box--the box into which guests can put checks, money, cards, etc. at a wedding reception. This particular design mimics the look of a tiered wedding cake or a stack of gifts. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-b1100a8c25')); Choose three gift boxes (or whatever number you prefer). The boxes you choose can be square or round, depending on your own preference or the wedding theme. The boxes should be in descending size, in order to create what will appear to be a stack. 2 Cut a slot in either the side or lid of the top box or in the side of the middle box. The box you choose is up to you; the top box will be easier for people to see, while the middle box will be larger. if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-4a4d7a8cd2')); The placement of the slot depends on how you're decorating the box and what you think will be more visually pleasing. Use a ruler and marker to trace out a rectangular shape either on the lid or side of the top box or on the side of the middle box. The rectangle should be slim but both long and wide enough to accommodate long envelopes. Cut out a neat rectangle shape. Use a craft knife to cut out the rectangle shape to form an envelope slot. A craft knife is preferable over scissors, which will leave ragged edges. 3 Measure the dimensions of the boxes. Use a ruler to measure length and width or each side of each gift box. [8] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-cf27828d28')); Write down the dimensions of each. 4 Cut decorative paper or fabric to fit over the sides of the boxes. Using the measurements for reference, lay the paper or fabric upside down on a flat surface. [9] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-d1e61d2db9')); Trace the dimensions of each side of each box onto the paper or fabric. If necessary, label the outlines so you’ll know which box they belong to. 5 Adhere the fabric or paper to the boxes. Spread a thin layer of glue on the back of each cutout and attach it to the corresponding part of each box. [10] X Research source if (WH.video)WH.video.add(document.getElementById('mvid-eba2928f99')); When the glue
